replies.author: @sarasunshinemt4444
replies.text:
What the Hollywood/Netflix people forget is that The Witcher show was being absolutely destroyed online with fans due to leaks of the writing, costumes and casting choices. Even the casting of Henry Cavill as Gerault was a hard pass for fans of the IP. 
Then came Henry's interview where he charmingly defended fans with their passionate, informed opinions. He came across as genuine and One Of Us, a fan, himself. 
So everyone paused, looked at Henry, realized he got what it was fans were concerned about, and basically asked for a chance to show us what he could do.
He freaking SAVED The Witcher from the very start. Professional critics barely watched it (and wrote that they were frequently confused, thanks to the director's story line jumping) but fans gave it a chance and made it the hit that season was.
Then, well, they spoiled all that good will and popularity with season 2 and onward by minimizing Gerault and bashing Henry every chance they got.
Self inflicted wounds, and it's past time for this body of work to embrace the rotting corpse that it is, and stop shambling around for relevance and viewers.

text:
The best thing is, sure in the books she was in a relationship with that girl, but you want to know how it started? She drugged Ciri with fissteh (basically the heroin of Witcher world) and with full power of romance and feminism, forced herself on her. Ciri was in such a vulnerable state because of the previous events, and spending weeks on a F*king desert she went along with it. And rats weren't some petty thieves. They were young bandits, they raided stuff and killed innocent people.
The plot with Emhyr was messed up on purpose too, but his reasons were different. He was Ciri's biological father, but since no one knew about it, he wanted to marry and have a child with her because of politics. This marriage would cement his position in the north. He didn't care at all for her powers, and since she was nowhere to be found, HE ordered to find a similar looking girl and get married to her while pretending she's Ciri...
Sorry for that, but subtle changes to the plot are usually more infuriating than radical ones...

​@matthiasschlachter1155  Ciri's sexuality is not particularly clear. It seems she was intended to be bisexual (or so Sapkowski seems to imply in some interviews), though when examining the books, it's not very clear. 

Obviously, her relationship with Mistle was sexual, though toxic. Otherwise, the only potential "evidence" is the fact that she thought an Elf and some sorceresses were beautiful. But to be honest, that is not itself strong evidence — since people regularly acknowledge beauty.

And Ciri's interests otherwise only seem to be towards men. She was "in love" with Hjalmar, infatuated with Jarl, and believed Cahir too pretty to kill. Eredin exploited her infatuation with him. She tried having sex with Hotspur, and ends The Lady of the Lake riding into the sunset holding the hand of Galahad, who gives her butterflies.

replies.author: @pusshi3936
replies.text: @LukiKruki there's also E) girls weren't further pursued because they are less capable of passing the trial of grasses AND being a witcher is just not a job fit for a woman. Gender roles are enforced in the world of the witcher, it's plausable they just deemed it not worth further pursuing. Of course Ciri is an exception, she's already shown resistance to the mind-washing water given to young druids so her having a resistance to the adverse effects of the trial of grasses is possible. I don't think she went through the Trial of Grasses to end herself, but more likely to A) continue to be a witcher after swearing off magic, which she does in the books and perhaps also does between the 3rd and 4th games. and B) to force her body to be infertale, which means she will no longer have children that will also carry the fate of the elder blood. It's said often that the major prophecies either refer to ciri, her child, or her grandchild. It could also be C) Since the trial of grasses directly effects the body's genetic makeup, maybe she used it as an attempt to muddle her elder blood and escape her fate. Hell, it could be up to all 3 reasons at the same time. But I definitely don't think it was a suicide attempt, the Ciri in the games and books may be rash, but not suicidal(except in the bad ending of the witcher 3, which is most definitely not the version of ciri in the witcher 4)

replies.author: @theresascholtz5298
replies.text: It was always going to be difficult transferring the message of the books to a show. In the books, Ciri, Yennefer and Geralt are almost never really together, and their real character development and motivations are explained/explored in the times they’re apart. I loved the books because each character was richly described, and developed in a way that would be hard to do in the limited timeframe of a tv show. The relationship between Geralt and Yennefer is described as being deeply loving and loyal, even though they didn’t always treat each other very well. Ciri had the natural progression from an immature child to a young adult who started to understand her power and responsibilities as she matured. I don’t see much of this in the show, and that’s disappointing. And I miss Henry C 😢

replies.author: @winry2357
replies.text: @theresascholtz5298yeah, I mean, it can be difficult to develop characters that aren’t together a lot, but the first season at least felt like they kinda tried. Another great example of connecting and developing a divided group of characters working toward the same goal would be Lord of the Rings. There were also three groups who all got roughly the same amount of screen time and all characters were well developed in the time span of three movies. That’s pretty much a masterclass in writing, but still… we should expect more. One Piece has a huge cast of characters that are all well developed and are often split up into groups.

author: @83gh
text:
To be fair, the plot points brought up here seem in line with the source material. Ciri does indeed join the Rats and starts a lesbian relationship with Mistle, although the show probably skips the fact that it starts with Mistle assaulting Ciri sexually, and from there it's basically grooming and active abuse, given Ciri's age at that point of the story in the books. The Rats do steal from the better-off and give to the poor, but they are never portrayed as having any sort of moral high ground - they represent young people who have been chewed in and spat out by their zeitgeist and decided to just do whatever they want, and not to give a fuck about anything, including whether they live or die. Finally, Emhyr being yet another party whose main motivation is to get Ciri and her Elder blood is (without giving out too much spoilers) the driving force behind a great number of events - past and future.

In terms of the language used, in a pre-word to one of his stories, Sapkowski says that he had his debut story returned to him by the editor, with his original modern language getting "antiquated", because the editor felt that "people didn't talk the way they do now back then". His response was that fantasy doesn't take place in any kind of "back then". Here's the translated excerpt:

[
The victims of the editor's blunder were primarily
phrases that are forbidden in fantasy because "they didn't say it that way back then." In the story published in "Fantastyka,"
I noticed with some astonishment that "conceit" had replaced
"arrogance," that "intelligent" had become "wise,"
etc. Since I staunchly defend the theory that fantasy doesn't take place
in any "back then," and that both archaizing the language and any stylization of the language are pointless, I eliminated Parowski's corrections from the version
you're about to read,
and returned to my original typescript.
]

Disclaimer: As an avid fan of the books, I only ever watched the first 20 minutes of S01E01 and gave up on the show.

I've read _The Wheel of Time_ and/or listened to the audiobooks completely at least 10 times, reading many of the earlier books closer to 20 times. I have absolutely no idea what you mean by "Girl Boss Promised Land."

*"And yet even given source material so completely in tune with "the message"..."*
LOL. Literally nothing in The Wheel of Time is in tune with THE MESSAGE. One of the primary themes in the series is that men and women are equal, and that both require the other to be complete. How many times are we told that the greatest works ever accomplished by the Aes Sedai required men and women working together? The tainting of _saidin_ happened explicitly because the female Aes Sedai thought Lews Therin's plan to reseal the Bore was too dangerous and refused to assist him. They were as much at fault for that disaster as Lews Therin was. The series shows us many examples of how women, particularly Aes Sedai, make a complete mess of things when they try to "girl boss." It also shows the same thing when men try to work alone. The idea that men and women have to work together is just as contrary to THE MESSAGE as showing men displaying any amount of masculinity and being right about anything.

@dreddwulf26 
*"Wheel of Time was entertaining..."*
Only in roughly the same way watching a train wreck is entertaining.

*"Not the books, but decent"*
You're kidding, right? The show was _obviously_ not the books...it wasn't even showing the same story as the books. Basically the only similarity between the books and the abomination Amazon created is names. Both the books and _The Wheel of Prime_ had characters named Rand al'Thor, Mat Cauthon, Perrin Aybara, Egwene al'Vere, and Nynaeve al'Meara from a place called the Two Rivers (though in the few episodes I watched, I never heard anyone use the actual name of the village, Emond's Field, implying "Two Rivers" was the village's name). None of the characters behaved remotely the way they were written in the novels, the events depicted had only a vague resemblance to the events described in the books, and the overall production was complete garbage, with costumes, locations, and characters looking nothing at all the way they were described in the novels. 

Anyone who says _The Wheel of Prime_ was anything other than a horrific abomination that should be destroyed in its entirety so no one else ever has to suffer through it can't have read / understood the books.

replies.author: @frocat5163
replies.text:
​@stanleyhumphrey7404 
*"His actions make little to no difference in the plot."*

Indiana Jones' actions in _Raiders of the Lost Ark_ make little to no difference to the plot, or at least to the plot's resolution. So, while I tend to agree that the story's alleged protagonist should have impact on the story, it isn't strictly necessary for the story to be good.

*" I dont think this was done for political reasons...if I had to guess i would just theorize that Sapkowski had a kink for cuckoldry and teenage lesbians"*

If it's not done for political reasons, then it is neither The Message nor Girl-Bossing. Particularly if it's written into the series as a cuckold fantasy. Both The Message and Girl-Bossing are done specifically to spread a socio-political dogma intent on "destroying the patriarchy." Males with a fetish/kink for cuckoldry or fem-dominance generally don't believe that women are superior to men; they just gain s3xual gratification from those things. A male with such a kink/fetish is no more likely to believe in the socio-political ethos that drives The Message and Girl-Bossing than a guy with a hygiene/shaving  fetish believes everyone should be completely bald and washed by other people.  

*"So what is this? is this  strong female characters or girl bossing? "*

As I said, I've not read the Witcher novels. Nor have I watched the show. Which is why I avoided making any specific claims about the show and how it compares to the books. In fact, I simply pointed out that the only adaptations that were successful met the criteria I listed, not that every adaptation that met those criteria were successful. 

*"whichever you choose, you at least can't say that it doesn't meet or exceed the drinkers description of the show."*

And I didn't say that. Because I haven't read the books or watched the show. My comment also wasn't specific to _The Witcher,_ but a comment on _multiple_ IPs adapted from book to film. Furthermore, by your own admission, you've not watched the show, either. Based on numerous videos on the subject, I'm pretty certain Drinker has both watched the show *AND* read the books, so between the two of you, I'm far more inclined to believe Drinker's evaluation than yours. And even if the points being referenced exist in both the books and the show, it's still entirely possible that the show's depiction is worse.

author: @themaster8432
text:
When i watched the first season of the Witcher (having played exactly 0 hours of any of the games, and read / seen exactly pages of any books or comics if there even are any), I was met with a show that perfectly explained the world in its entirety.. What is a witcher, what makes them special, how is the magic working in this fantasy world (its never the same in two universes), and i really really liked it.. When Gerald met Siri for the first time in that forest and then subsequently utterly destroyed the entire Witcher home base. I was excited for the future of this show.. Then i booted up the first episode of the next season and saw the clownshow of "characters" that poluted the screen for the first 10 minutes, and decided this was not gonna be a season that i wanted to continue watching. 

As a fantasy loving person, I watched the episode of Game of thrones where John snow encountered the night king for the first time, where that wildling village was eradicated within a few short minutes, and the chilling scene where snow floats slowly away from shore on that boat and the night king just raises his arms and the dead rise. The most horrifying and amazing scene in all of fantasy cinema ever to grace the screens. 
I was having similar hopes for the witcher.. the potential is there. The stange monsters and magic are so awesome that it could have been the most Legendary show ever.. 

Lets be honest what fantasy show with Magic do we have? Harry potter? pffff please, spare me the childish drama of wands and magic words. Lord of the rings? pff they barely use magic at all, and when they do its so subtle that it could just as well be no magic at all. And now I have exhaused the list of fantasy movies / series with actual magic in it.. (that i know of). So im hungry for the Witcher level magic and mages, and monsters. Its beyond even the magic of Game of thrones. 

to fuck this series up, is unforgivable. and doing it on purpose, should be punishable by death imo.

replies.author: @BassPlay3r
replies.text:
Whoa Yes, Ciri was sexually assaulted by members of The Rats in Andrzej Sapkowski's books. After being rescued from mercenaries, one of the Rats, Kayleigh, attempts to sexually assault her. Mistle, another member of the group, intervenes to stop Kayleigh but then proceeds to sexually assault Ciri herself, with no clear indication that Ciri consented to the encounter.
 This sequence of events is widely regarded as a traumatic and controversial moment in the narrative, contributing to Ciri's psychological and emotional transformation during her time with the group.
 The assault is described as occurring while Ciri is in a state of fear and shock, and she later remains with the Rats not out of desire, but due to her desperate need to avoid being alone.
 The incident is a pivotal point in Ciri's arc, marking the beginning of her descent into a more morally ambiguous and violent persona.

replies.text:
Do not sugarcoat it, Mistle literally raped Ciri in the beginning and held her (emotionally) hostage until she developed a mild Stockholm-syndrome. That's a textbook abusive relationship. She even states outright that Ciri is only ever nice when she wants something and goes along with the sex to get it. 

 Ciri’s relationship (if you want to call it that, a huge stretch though) was because she was being used due to how vulnerable and broken she was. 
 In the books, it highlights the dark place she was in AND how cruel the Rats were, including getting her addicted to the Witcher’s version of heroin and slaughtering people for pleasure. 

 At one point, the Rats are working for a nobleman guy and they let his daughter go, as per the terms. Ciri, at this point in her life, is ENRAGED at this action, screaming at them that “The wench should have been ravaged!” 

 Yeah, she wanted to likely RAPE and kill the girl… because she could.
